items.prompt: What is the result of logically right-shifting the 8-bit unsigned pattern 10010000 by 2 positions?

feedback.reason: Logical right shift inserts zeros at the high end.
feedback.details: Shifting 10010000 right twice yields 00100100.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bits_11100100: That is the arithmetic-right-shift result with sign extension.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bits_01001000: This is a shift by one position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bits_00010010: This is a shift by three positions.

constraints: Use exactly 8 bits and logical right shift.

items.prompt: What is the result of arithmetically right-shifting the 8-bit two's-complement pattern 10010000 by 2 positions?

feedback.reason: Arithmetic right shift replicates the sign bit.
feedback.details: The leading one is copied into the two vacated high positions, preserving a negative two's-complement interpretation.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bits_00100100: That fills high positions with zeros rather than the sign bit.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bits_11001000: This is only one arithmetic shift.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bits_10010000: A nonzero shift changes bit positions.

constraints: Use exactly 8 bits, two's-complement interpretation, and arithmetic right shift.

items.prompt: For which values do logical and arithmetic right shift produce the same high-bit fill under a fixed-width two's-complement model?

options.text: Values whose sign bit is zero.
All negative values.
Every value.
Only zero.

feedback.reason: Arithmetic right shift copies the sign bit, while logical right shift always inserts zero.
feedback.details: When the sign bit is zero, both fill rules are identical.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.negative: Arithmetic shift inserts ones while logical shift inserts zeros.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_values: They differ for values with the sign bit set.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_zero: Every nonnegative pattern receives zero fill in both operations.

items.prompt: When does left-shifting a nonnegative integer x by k represent multiplication by 2^k?

options.text: When the declared representation can hold the result without discarding significant bits.
For every fixed-width signed value, including overflow.
Only when x is negative.
Only when k is at least the word width.

feedback.reason: A left shift moves every one bit to a place value multiplied by 2^k.
feedback.details: The arithmetic equivalence holds only when the representation preserves all shifted bits.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_signed: Truncation or overflow can change the mathematical result.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.negative_only: The statement is simplest for nonnegative values.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.shift_large: Such counts are outside this pack's valid shift contract.

constraints: Shift count is within the word width.

items.prompt: What is the 8-bit result of left-shifting 10110001 by 1 position, discarding overflow beyond bit 7?

feedback.reason: Left shift moves every bit one position toward the high end and inserts zero at bit 0.
feedback.details: The original high bit leaves the 8-bit word and is discarded.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bits_10110010: That changes only the low bit rather than shifting all positions.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bits_11100010: The high overflow bit is discarded, not copied back.

constraints: Use exactly 8 bits. Discard bits shifted beyond position 7.

items.prompt: Why is arithmetic right shift of a negative two's-complement integer not universally equivalent to integer division that truncates toward zero?

options.text: Arithmetic right shift commonly rounds negative odd values toward negative infinity, not toward zero.
Right shift always changes the integer width.
Arithmetic right shift is defined only for unsigned values.
Right shift performs XOR internally.

feedback.reason: For example, arithmetic -3 shifted right by one yields -2 in a sign-extending model.
feedback.details: Division by 2 with truncation toward zero would yield -1, so the operations differ.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.different_width: The operation can remain within one fixed width.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_unsigned: Its purpose is sign-preserving shift of signed patterns.

constraints: Use arithmetic right shift with sign extension and compare against division truncating toward zero.

items.prompt: What is the effect of sign-extending the 8-bit two's-complement pattern 11110000 to 16 bits?

feedback.reason: Signed widening repeats the original sign bit in all new high positions.
feedback.details: This preserves the two's-complement numeric value.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bits_0000000011110000: Zero extension changes the signed numeric value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bits_1111000000000000: Widening does not shift the original bits.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bits_0000111100001111: Sign extension repeats the sign bit rather than complementing sections.

items.prompt: What should shifting any fixed-width word by zero positions do?

options.text: Return the unchanged bit pattern.
Return all zeros.
Reverse bit order.
Keep only the sign bit.

feedback.reason: Zero is the identity shift count.
feedback.details: This edge case should be handled without introducing width or sign changes.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sign_only: A zero shift preserves every bit.

items.prompt: Why does this pack require shift counts to satisfy 0 <= k < wordWidth for fixed-width questions?

options.text: Behavior for counts at or beyond the width varies across languages and machine models.
Large shift counts cannot be represented numerically.
Every language guarantees a zero result for large shifts.
Every language throws an error.

feedback.reason: Portable reasoning needs an explicit shift-count contract.
feedback.details: Restricting k to the valid bit-position range avoids silently depending on language-specific masking or error behavior.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_large_numbers: They can be represented, but their semantics vary.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_zero: Some mask the count, trap, or define other behavior.

items.prompt: Which statements about logical right shift are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: Vacated high positions are filled with zeros.
For a nonnegative unsigned value, shifting by k equals floor(value / 2^k).
It always preserves a negative signed value's sign.
The k lowest bits are discarded.

feedback.reason: Logical right shift is a zero-filling positional operation.
feedback.details: Signed interpretation after the shift may differ substantially from the original sign.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sign_preserve: Zero fill can turn the high sign bit off.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.unsigned_floor: Low discarded bits form the remainder.

items.prompt: Which statements about arithmetic right shift are correct under the declared two's-complement model? Select all that apply.

options.text: Vacated high positions copy the original sign bit.
For nonnegative values, it matches logical right shift.
For negative odd values, it may differ from division that truncates toward zero.
It always inserts zeros.

feedback.reason: Arithmetic shift preserves the sign bit pattern rather than always performing language-level division.
feedback.details: Its numeric interpretation depends on two's-complement signed semantics.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.negative_rounding: Sign extension corresponds to floor-like behavior.

constraints: Use fixed-width two's-complement arithmetic right shift.

items.prompt: Which conditions are needed before treating x shifted left by k as exact multiplication by 2^k? Select all that apply.

options.text: The numeric interpretation is one for which the shift operation is explicitly defined.
The shift count is inside the declared width contract.
No significant one bit is discarded beyond the available width.
Overflow or truncation is allowed without changing the arithmetic claim.

feedback.reason: The arithmetic shortcut is conditional on a stable representation model and sufficient width.
feedback.details: Bit movement itself is deterministic only after those assumptions are fixed.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.any_overflow: Discarded high bits break exact multiplication.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.nonnegative_or_defined_model: Signed negative semantics vary by model.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.no_significant_loss: Otherwise truncation changes the mathematical value.

items.prompt: Which effects can occur during fixed-width left shift? Select all that apply.

options.text: Zeros enter the vacated low positions.
High bits can be discarded beyond the word width.
The signed interpretation can change sign after truncation.
Every original bit is always preserved somewhere inside the word.

feedback.reason: Fixed-width left shift is a bit-pattern operation with truncation.
feedback.details: Arithmetic interpretation must account for lost high bits and the new sign position.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.preserve_all: High bits may leave the word.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.zero_low: Left shift always fills low positions with zero.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sign_change: The new most significant bit may differ.

items.prompt: Which assumptions should be stated for portable shift reasoning? Select all that apply.

options.text: The word width.
Whether the value is interpreted as signed or unsigned.
Whether right shift is logical or arithmetic.
The name of the shifted variable.

feedback.reason: Shift behavior is not fully specified by the symbols alone across programming environments.
feedback.details: Width, signedness, and fill semantics make the bit model reviewable.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.word_width: It determines valid positions and truncation.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.signedness: Right-shift and numeric meaning may differ.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.right_shift_kind: The fill rule must be explicit.

items.prompt: Arrange the steps for logically right-shifting a fixed-width bit pattern by k.

elements.text: Discard the k least significant positions.
Move every remaining bit k positions toward the least significant end.
Fill the k vacated high positions with zeros.
Interpret the resulting fixed-width pattern under the stated signedness.

feedback.reason: Logical right shift is defined by positional movement and zero fill.
feedback.details: Numeric interpretation is a separate final step.

items.prompt: Arrange the steps for arithmetically right-shifting a negative two's-complement pattern.

elements.text: Observe that the most significant bit is one.
Move the retained bits toward the least significant end.
Fill vacated high positions with ones.

feedback.reason: Arithmetic shift copies the original sign bit into every new high position.
feedback.details: The operation remains width-dependent even though the sign is preserved.

items.prompt: Arrange the steps for evaluating a fixed-width left shift safely.

elements.text: Confirm the shift count is within the declared contract.
Move each bit k positions toward the high end.
Discard bits that leave the word.
Fill the vacated low positions with zeros.

feedback.reason: Fixed-width left shift combines positional movement with truncation.
feedback.details: Arithmetic claims should be evaluated only after the final pattern is known.

items.prompt: Arrange the process for deciding whether a right shift can replace division by 2^k.

elements.text: State signedness and the right-shift fill rule.
State the required division rounding rule.
Check whether negative values are possible.
Use the shift only if both operations agree for the entire allowed domain.

feedback.reason: The substitution is a semantic claim, not merely a performance trick.
feedback.details: Negative values and rounding direction are the critical boundary.

items.prompt: On a fixed-width machine word, one valid shift and one mask operation are performed. Select time and auxiliary-space complexity.
constraints: The word width is a machine constant.
The shift count is within the valid range.

feedback.reason: Primitive shifts and masks are constant-time under the fixed-word model.
feedback.details: This model should not be generalized automatically to arbitrary-precision integers.

items.prompt: An arbitrary-precision integer has w stored bits. A left shift by k requires moving or rewriting the represented limbs. Select a width-sensitive time bound and additional result space.
constraints: w is part of the input size.
The shifted result is materialized separately and has O(w + k) bits.

feedback.reason: Arbitrary-precision shifts scale with the represented width and result size.
feedback.details: The fixed-machine-word O(1) assumption no longer applies.

items.prompt: A positive w-bit integer is repeatedly logically right-shifted by one until it becomes zero. Select the number of iterations and auxiliary-space complexity.
constraints: The most significant set bit is at position w - 1.
Only the current word and a counter are stored.

feedback.reason: Each shift removes one binary place from the remaining magnitude.
feedback.details: The number of iterations is proportional to bit length, not numeric value.

items.prompt: A function performs q valid shifts on one fixed-width word, with O(1) work per shift. Select total time and auxiliary space.
constraints: The q shift requests are processed sequentially.
No history of intermediate words is retained.

feedback.reason: Constant-time primitives accumulate across q requested operations.
feedback.details: Only the current fixed-width word and loop state are retained.
